(東京スカイツリー, *Tōkyō Sukaitsurī*; [[toːkʲoː sɯ̥kaitsɯriː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:TomJ-TokyoSkytree.ogg "File:TomJ-TokyoSkytree.ogg")), a.k.a **Tokyo Sky Tree**, is a broadcasting and observation [tower](/wiki/Tower "Tower"), located in [Sumida](/wiki/Sumida,_Tokyo "Sumida, Tokyo"), [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), Japan. It has been the [tallest tower](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures_in_Japan "List of tallest structures in Japan") in Japan since opening in 2012,[[2]](#cite_note-tallest-2) and reached its full height of 634 metres (2,080 feet) in early 2011, making it the [tallest tower](/wiki/List_of_tallest_towers_in_the_world "List of tallest towers in the world") in the world, displacing the [Canton Tower](/wiki/Canton_Tower "Canton Tower"),[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) and the third [tallest structure](/wiki/Tallest_structure "Tallest structure") in the world behind [Merdeka 118](/wiki/Merdeka_118 "Merdeka 118") (678.9 metres or 2,227 feet) and [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa "Burj Khalifa") (829.8 m or 2,722 ft).[[5]](#cite_note-SecondTallest-5)[[a]](#cite_note-6)

**Tokyo Skytree** (東京スカイツリー, *Tōkyō Sukaitsurī*; [[toːkʲoː sɯ̥kaitsɯriː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:TomJ-TokyoSkytree.ogg "File:TomJ-TokyoSkytree.ogg")), a.k.a **Tokyo Sky Tree**, is a broadcasting and observation [tower](/wiki/Tower "Tower"), located in [Sumida](/wiki/Sumida,_Tokyo "Sumida, Tokyo"), [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), Japan. It has been the [tallest tower](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures_in_Japan "List of tallest structures in Japan") in Japan since opening in 2012,[[2]](#cite_note-tallest-2) and reached its full height of 634 metres (2,080 feet) in early 2011, making it the [tallest tower](/wiki/List_of_tallest_towers_in_the_world "List of tallest towers in the world") in the world, displacing the [Canton Tower](/wiki/Canton_Tower "Canton Tower"),[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) and the third [tallest structure](/wiki/Tallest_structure "Tallest structure") in the world behind [Merdeka 118](/wiki/Merdeka_118 "Merdeka 118") (678.9 metres or 2,227 feet) and [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa "Burj Khalifa") (829.8 m or 2,722 ft).[[5]](#cite_note-SecondTallest-5)[[a]](#cite_note-6)

The tower is the primary television and radio broadcast site for the [Kantō region](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region "Kantō region"); the older [Tokyo Tower](/wiki/Tokyo_Tower "Tokyo Tower") no longer gives complete [digital terrestrial television](/wiki/Digital_terrestrial_television "Digital terrestrial television") broadcasting coverage because it is surrounded by high-rise buildings. Skytree was completed on Leap Day, 29 February 2012, with the tower opening to the public on 22 May 2012.[[6]](#cite_note-Tokyo_Skytree-7) The tower is the centerpiece of a large commercial development funded by [Tobu Railway](/wiki/Tobu_Railway "Tobu Railway") (which owns the complex) and a group of six terrestrial broadcasters headed by [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK"). Trains stop at the adjacent [Tokyo Skytree Station](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree_Station "Tokyo Skytree Station") and nearby [Oshiage Station](/wiki/Oshiage_Station "Oshiage Station"). The complex is seven kilometres (4.3 miles) northeast of [Tokyo Station](/wiki/Tokyo_Station "Tokyo Station"). [Sumida Aquarium](/wiki/Sumida_Aquarium "Sumida Aquarium") is in the Tokyo Solamachi complex.

## Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Skytree&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Design")]

The tower's design was published on 24 November 2006,[[7]](#cite_note-8) based on the following three concepts:

* Fusion of [neofuturistic](/wiki/Neofuturistic "Neofuturistic")[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10) design and the traditional beauty of Japan
* Catalyst for revitalization of the city
* Contribution to disaster prevention – "Safety and Security"

The base of the tower has a structure similar to a [tripod](/wiki/Tripod "Tripod"); from a height of about 350 m (1,150 ft) and above, the tower's structure is cylindrical to offer panoramic views of the river and the city.[[10]](#cite_note-11) There are observatories at 350 m (1,150 ft), with a capacity of up to 2,000 people, and 450 m (1,480 ft), with a capacity of 900 people.[[11]](#cite_note-12) The upper observatory features a spiral, glass-covered skywalk in which visitors ascend the last 5 m (16 ft) to the highest point at the upper platform. A section of glass flooring gives visitors a direct downward view of the streets below.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

### Earthquake resistance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Skytree&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Earthquake resistance")]

The tower has [seismic proofing](/wiki/Earthquake_engineering_structures "Earthquake engineering structures"), including a central shaft made of reinforced concrete. The main internal pillar is attached to the outer tower structure for the first 125 m (410 ft) above ground. From there until 375 m (1,230 ft), the pillar is attached to the tower frame with oil dampers, which act as cushions during an earthquake. Additional resilience is achieved through an "added mass control mechanism" (or [tuned mass damper](/wiki/Tuned_mass_damper "Tuned mass damper")) – a damping system which, in the event of an earthquake, moves out of step with the building's structure, to keep the center of gravity as central as possible to the tower's base.[[13]](#cite_note-14) According to the designers, the dampers can absorb 50 percent of the energy from an earthquake.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16)

### Color

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Skytree&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Color")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Tokyo_Skytree_at_night_%28Iki%29.jpg/250px-Tokyo_Skytree_at_night_%28Iki%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Skytree_at_night_(Iki).jpg)

*Iki*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Tokyo_Skytree_at_night_%28Miyabi%29.jpg/250px-Tokyo_Skytree_at_night_%28Miyabi%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Skytree_at_night_(Miyabi).jpg)

The exterior lattice is painted a color officially called "Skytree White". This is an original color based on a bluish-white [traditional Japanese color](/wiki/Traditional_colors_of_Japan "Traditional colors of Japan") called *aijiro* (藍白).[[16]](#cite_note-colordesign-17)

### Illumination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Skytree&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Illumination")]

The illumination design was published on 16 October 2009. Two illumination patterns alternate daily. One is the sky blue *[Iki](/wiki/Iki_(aesthetics) "Iki (aesthetics)")* (chic, stylish), and the other is the purple *[Miyabi](/wiki/Miyabi "Miyabi")* (elegance, refinement). The tower is illuminated using [LEDs](/wiki/Light-emitting_diode "Light-emitting diode").[[17]](#cite_note-18)

## Naming and height

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Skytree&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Naming and height")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Tokyo_Skytree_Silhouette.svg/250px-Tokyo_Skytree_Silhouette.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Skytree_Silhouette.svg)

The [cross-section](/wiki/Cross_section_(geometry) "Cross section (geometry)") of the tower forms an [equilateral triangle](/wiki/Equilateral_triangle "Equilateral triangle") on the ground, gradually rounding to become circular at 320 m elevation.

From October to November 2007, suggestions were collected from the general public for the name to be given to the tower. On 19 March 2008, a committee chose six final candidate names: *Tōkyō Sukaitsurī* (東京スカイツリー; "Tokyo sky tree"), *Tōkyō Edo Tawā* (東京EDOタワー; "Tokyo Edo tower"), *Raijingu Tawā* (ライジングタワー; "Rising tower"), *Mirai Tawā* (みらいタワー; "Tower of the future"), *Yumemi Yagura* (ゆめみやぐら; "Dream lookout"), *Raijingu Īsuto Tawā* (ライジングイーストタワー; "Rising east tower"). The official name was decided in a nationwide vote, and was announced on 10 June 2008 as "Tokyo Skytree". The name received around 33,000 votes (30%) out of 110,000 cast, with the second most popular name being "Tokyo [Edo](/wiki/Edo "Edo") Tower".[[18]](#cite_note-19)

The height of 634 m (2,080 ft) was selected to be easily remembered based on [numeric substitution](/wiki/Numeric_substitution_in_Japanese "Numeric substitution in Japanese"). The figures 6 (mu), 3 (sa), 4 (shi) stand for "[Musashi](/wiki/Musashi_Province "Musashi Province")", an old name for the region where the Tokyo Skytree stands.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

## Timeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Skytree&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Timeline")]

### 2008

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Skytree&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2008")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Tokyo_Sky_Tree_under_construction_%284121825262%29.jpg/250px-Tokyo_Sky_Tree_under_construction_%284121825262%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Sky_Tree_under_construction_(4121825262).jpg)

Tokyo Skytree under construction in November 2009

* 14 July 2008: A ceremony was held at the site to mark the start of construction.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

### 2009

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Skytree&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2009")]

* 6 April 2009: The [foundations](/wiki/Foundation_(engineering) "Foundation (engineering)") for the three main legs were completed.[[21]](#cite_note-22)
* 7 August 2009: The tower reached a height of 100 m.[[22]](#cite_note-23)
* 16 October 2009: The projected height was increased from 610 to 634 m (2,001 to 2,080 ft) to make it the highest self-supporting[*[further explanation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] steel tower. Also, 6-3-4 is [*Mu-sa-shi*](/wiki/Musashi_Province "Musashi Province") in [Japanese wordplay](/wiki/Japanese_wordplay "Japanese wordplay") *goroawase*.[[23]](#cite_note-24)
* 10 November 2009: The tower reached a height of 200 m.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

### 2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Skytree&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2010")]

* 16 February 2010: The tower reached a height of 300 m (980 ft).[[25]](#cite_note-26)
* 29 March 2010: The tower reached a height of 338 m (1,109 ft), becoming the tallest structure in Japan.[[2]](#cite_note-tallest-2)
* 24 April 2010: A 1:25 scale model of the Tokyo Skytree was unveiled at the [Tobu World Square](/wiki/Tobu_World_Square "Tobu World Square") theme park in [Nikkō, Tochigi](/wiki/Nikk%C5%8D,_Tochigi "Nikkō, Tochigi").[[26]](#cite_note-27)
* 30 July 2010: The tower topped 400 m (1,300 ft), reaching a height of 413 m (1,355 ft).[[27]](#cite_note-28)
* 11 September 2010: The tower reached 461 m (1,512 ft), becoming the tallest structure ever built in Japan, surpassing the dismantled [Tsushima Omega tower](/wiki/Omega_(navigation_system)#Omega_Tower,_Tsushima "Omega (navigation system)") of 455 m (1,493 ft).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* 23 October 2010: The tower reached a height of 497 m (1,631 ft), and assembly of the main tower section was completed.
* 20 November 2010: Two [tuned mass dampers](/wiki/Tuned_mass_damper "Tuned mass damper") with a total weight of 100 [tons](/wiki/Tonne "Tonne") were temporarily placed on the tower tip at 497 m (1,631 ft).[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30)
* 1 December 2010: The tower topped the 500 metres (1,600 ft) mark and reached a height of 511 m (1,677 ft), beating [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101 "Taipei 101") (509 m (1,670 ft)). A lightning conductor and two tuned mass dampers were docked to the gain tower, which was gradually lifted within the central shaft.[[30]](#cite_note-31)
* 16 December 2010: [Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications](/wiki/Ministry_of_Internal_Affairs_and_Communications "Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications") approved [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK") and [five TV key stations in Tokyo](/wiki/Zaiky%C5%8D_k%C4%AB_kyoku "Zaikyō kī kyoku")'s plans to install their broadcasting facilities on the tower.[[31]](#cite_note-32)
* 18 December 2010: The [transmitting](/wiki/Transmitter "Transmitter") [antenna](/wiki/Antenna_(radio) "Antenna (radio)") for digital terrestrial television began to be installed.

### 2011

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Skytree&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2011")]

* 1 March 2011: The tower topped the 600-metre (1,969 ft) mark and reached a height of 602 m (1,975 ft), surpassing [Canton Tower](/wiki/Canton_Tower "Canton Tower") and becoming the world's [tallest tower](/wiki/List_of_tallest_towers_in_the_world "List of tallest towers in the world").[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34)
* 12 March 2011: The tower reached a height of 625 m (2,051 ft). A full inspection was made, looking for possible damage by the [2011 Tōhoku earthquake](/wiki/2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami "2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami") and its [aftershocks](/wiki/Aftershock "Aftershock").
* 18 March 2011: The tower reached its final height of 634 m (2,080 ft) at 1:34 pm [JST](/wiki/Japan_Standard_Time "Japan Standard Time").[[34]](#cite_note-35)
* 23 May 2011: Dismantling four [tower cranes](/wiki/Crane_(machine) "Crane (machine)") continues until mid-July.[[35]](#cite_note-36)
* 7 June 2011: Announced [public opening](/wiki/Grand_opening "Grand opening") date of Tokyo Skytree Town and entrance fee (Adults: 2,000 yen to 350 m (1,150 ft) level; extra 1,000 yen to 450 m (1,480 ft) level) to [observation floors](/wiki/Observation_deck "Observation deck").[[36]](#cite_note-37)
* 17 November 2011: Guinness World Records certified the Tokyo Skytree as the tallest free-standing tower.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

### 2012

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Skytree&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2012")]

* 16 February 2012: The roofs of warehouses close to the tower were damaged by falling snow and ice from the tower.[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40)
* 29 February 2012: Tower construction was finished. Completion was delayed two months from the original schedule because of a shortage of supplies due to the [effects of the 2011 Japanese earthquake and tsunami](/wiki/Aftermath_of_the_2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami "Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami").[[40]](#cite_note-41)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]
* 2 March 2012: A ceremony was held to celebrate the completion with a *[kannushi](/wiki/Kannushi "Kannushi")* priest and 70 people from [Tobu Group](/wiki/Tobu_Railway "Tobu Railway"), construction, broadcasting and other companies.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43)
* 6 March 2012: First Light-up during the Tokyo Hotaru Festival
* 26 April 2012: [Emperor Akihito](/wiki/Emperor_Akihito "Emperor Akihito") and [Empress Michiko](/wiki/Empress_Michiko "Empress Michiko") of Japan inspected the Tokyo Skytree Tembo Galleria.[[43]](#cite_note-44)
* 22 May 2012: Public opening of Tokyo Skytree
* 1 October 2012: Channel 9 [Tokyo MX](/wiki/Tokyo_MX "Tokyo MX") start transmission from Tokyo Skytree with continuing transmission from [Tokyo Tower](/wiki/Tokyo_Tower "Tokyo Tower") in [simulcast](/wiki/Simulcast "Simulcast") manner.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

### 2013

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Skytree&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 2013")]

* 16 January 2013: Snow falling from the tower knocked a hole in the roof of a nearby house. No one was reported injured.[[45]](#cite_note-46)
* 13 May 2013: [Tokyo MX](/wiki/Tokyo_MX "Tokyo MX") continued transmission from Tokyo Skytree and stopped transmission from Tokyo Tower with a gradual decrease in power since 12 November 2012.[[46]](#cite_note-47)
* 31 May 2013: On 9:00 a.m., formal transmission of broadcast in channel 1 to 8, except 3, start from Tokyo Skytree after number of test transmission with off for minutes to hours from [Tokyo Tower](/wiki/Tokyo_Tower "Tokyo Tower") since 22 December 2012.

### 2022

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Skytree&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 2022")]

* 22 May 2022: the 10th anniversary of the opening of Tokyo Skytree was celebrated with [Kabuki](/wiki/Kabuki "Kabuki") by [Ichikawa Ebizo XI](/wiki/Ichikawa_Ebizo_XI "Ichikawa Ebizo XI") who performed a signature technique called the "nirami" glare on a special stage atop of the tower.[[47]](#cite_note-kabuki-48)

## Opening

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Skytree&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Opening")]

As the Skytree's opening approached, people reportedly waited in line for a week to get tickets. By the opening, trips up the tower were fully booked for the first two months of operation.[[48]](#cite_note-Time225-49) The opening day drew a crowd of tens of thousands, despite rainy conditions which blocked the view from the tower's [observation deck](/wiki/Observation_deck "Observation deck"). Strong winds also forced two elevators to be shut down, leaving some visitors briefly stranded on the observation deck.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

According to Tobu, 1.6 million people visited Skytree in its first week. Local residents reported that the influx of visitors disturbed the peace of their community and had, so far, generated little economic benefit for the local area.[[50]](#cite_note-51)
